---
author: Emad Saberi
title: Demonstrating DSGE modelling with MacroModelling.jl
date: 2026/05/11
format:
 html:
  embed-resources: true
  theme: simplex
---

_For presentation to Dr. Giacomo Candian_

Paper: [Loss Function-Based Evaluation of DSGE Models (Frank Schorfheide, 2000)](http://proxy2.hec.ca/login?url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2678564)

# Paper Summary

Given DSGE models are almost certainly misspecified, how to coherently and formally evaluate and compare them?

Schorfheide proposes evaluating DSGE models through Loss Functions, compares Cash in Advance and Portfolio Adjustment Cost. VAR serves as flexible reference model

1. Compute posterior distributions via MH
2. Derive posteriors over population characteristics as a weighted mixture across the models
3. Evaluate three loss functions and rank model predictions
4. Find loss-function based parameter estimates that achieve best fit

# Reproduction, Framework

Only focused on reproducing **CIA Model** with the **MacroModelling.jl** package, goal is to estimate posterior distributions of parameters and compare with paper.


```{julia}
#| label: "julia and package version info"
pwd()
run(`julia --version`)
using Pkg; Pkg.activate(".."); Pkg.status()
```

## MacroModelling.jl: background

[Github link](https://github.com/thorek1/MacroModelling.jl)
[Workshop link](https://www.thorekockerols.eu/workshop.html)
[Official Documentation](https://www.thorekockerols.eu/MacroModelling.jl/stable/)

### Description

`MacroModelling.jl` is a Julia package for developing and solving dynamic stochastic general equilibrium (DSGE) models. v0.1 released 2023.

- `MacroModelling.jl` serves as a tool for handling the complexities involved with DSGE modelling (forward-looking expectations, nonlinearity, and high dimensionality).
- The goal is to reduce coding time and speed up model development by providing functions for working with discrete-time DSGE models. 
- The package allows the user to work with nonlinear model solutions ... and estimate the model using gradient based samplers (e.g. NUTS, or HMC).
- Target audience includes central bankers, regulators, graduate students, and others working in academia with an interest in DSGE modelling.

### The Author

[Thore Kockerols](https://www.thorekockerols.eu/)

Senior Economist at European Central Bank, PhD Econ (2018 Universite Paris Sarbonne)

[Package Presentation at JuliaCon24](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4NkJBDVxMWo)

Area of research and latest publications:
[Leaning Against the Wind: Macroprudential Policy and the Financial Cycle](https://doi.org/10.2866/744981)

## Replication

```{julia}

# %% Setup environment
using Pkg
cd(joinpath(@__DIR__,".."))
Pkg.activate(".")

# %% prep data
# include("data/dataprep.jl")

# %%
using CSV, DataFrames, AxisKeys, MacroModelling, PrettyTables
```

```{julia}

# %% load data
dat = CSV.read("assets/data/FS2000_data.csv", DataFrame);
pretty_table(describe(dat))
```

```{julia}

# %% Transform to KeyedArray
data = KeyedArray(Array(dat)',
    Variable = Symbol.("log_".*names(dat)),
    Time= 1:size(dat,1)
);

# %% logs
data = log.(data);

# %% declare observables
observables = sort(Symbol.("log_".*names(dat)));
data = data(observables,:)
describe(data)
```

```{julia}
# %% define model
@model FS2000 begin
    dA[0] = exp(γ + z_e_a*e_a[x])
    log(m[0]) = (1 - ρ)*log(mst)  +  ρ*log(m[-1]) + z_e_m*e_m[x]

    - P[0] / (c[1]*P[1]*m[0]) + β*P[1]*(α*exp(-α*(γ + log(e[1])))*k[0] ^ (α - 1)*n[1] ^ (1 - α) + (1 - δ)*exp( - (γ + log(e[1])))) / (c[2]*P[2]*m[1]) = 0

    W[0] = l[0] / n[0]
    - (ψ / (1 - ψ))*(c[0]*P[0] / (1 - n[0])) + l[0] / n[0] = 0

    R[0] = P[0]*(1 - α)*exp( - α*(γ + z_e_a*e_a[x]))*k[-1] ^ α*n[0] ^ ( - α) / W[0]

    1 / (c[0]*P[0]) - β*P[0]*(1 - α)*exp( - α*(γ + z_e_a*e_a[x]))*k[-1] ^ α*n[0] ^ (1 - α) / (m[0]*l[0]*c[1]*P[1]) = 0

    c[0] + k[0] = exp( - α*(γ + z_e_a*e_a[x]))*k[-1] ^ α*n[0] ^ (1 - α) + (1 - δ)*exp( - (γ + z_e_a*e_a[x]))*k[-1]

    P[0]*c[0] = m[0]
    m[0] - 1 + d[0] = l[0]
    e[0] = exp(z_e_a*e_a[x])
    y[0] = k[-1] ^ α*n[0] ^ (1 - α)*exp( - α*(γ + z_e_a*e_a[x]))

    gy_obs[0] = dA[0]*y[0] / y[-1]
    gp_obs[0] = (P[0] / P[-1])*m[-1] / dA[0]
    log_gy_obs[0] = log(gy_obs[0])
    log_gp_obs[0] = log(gp_obs[0])

end
```

```{julia}
# %% define parameters
@parameters FS2000 begin
    α     = 0.356
    β     = 0.993
    γ     = 0.0085
    mst   = 1.0002
    ρ     = 0.129
    ψ     = 0.65
    δ     = 0.01
    z_e_a = 0.035449
    z_e_m = 0.008862
end
```

```{julia}
# %% Sampling imports
import Turing
import Turing: NUTS, sample, logpdf, replacenames

import ADTypes: AutoZygote
import Zygote
```

Note: Turing.jl is the julia workhorse for probabilistic programming and sampling. MacroModelling.jl is designed to work well with Turing. A solved MacroModel provides a defined log likelihood function which feeds into Turing nicely.

Turing itself, in turn, works well with Zygote.jl: the principle automatic differentiating engine in Julia. Zygote enables the No U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) that automatically chooses the optimal step size for the sampler, and allows for convergence in significantly fewer samples than Metropolis Hastings. 

Workflow becomes: 
1. Define model, parameters, solve for steady state, linearize with MacroModelling.jl. MacroModelling.jl provides a kalman-filter log likelihood function with `get_loglikelihood(mode, data, parameters)`
2. Feed the custom log likelihood function to Turing.jl. Assuming the solved model's likelihood is first order differentiable, specify the sampler as NUTS
3. Select ~1000 samples with NUTS to describe posterior space with

```{julia}
#| output: false
# %% define prior dists
prior_dists = [
    Beta(0.356, 0.02, μσ = true),           # \alpha
    Beta(0.993, 0.002, μσ = true),          # \beta
    Normal(0.0085, 0.003),                  # \gamma
    Normal(1.0002, 0.007),                  # mst
    Beta(0.129, 0.223, μσ = true),          # \rho
    Beta(0.65, 0.05, μσ = true),            # \psi
    Beta(0.01, 0.005, μσ = true),           # \delta
    InverseGamma(0.035449, Inf, μσ = true), # z_e_a
    InverseGamma(0.008862, Inf, μσ = true)  # z_e_m
]
```

^ From Schorfheide 2000

```{julia}

#| output: false
# %% define sampling model
Turing.@model function FS2000_loglik_func(prior_dists, data, m; verbose=false)

    parameters ~ Turing.arraydist(prior_dists)

    Turing.@addlogprob! get_loglikelihood(m, data, parameters)
end

# %% Fit priors, data, model
FS2000_loglik = FS2000_loglik_func(prior_dists, data, FS2000) ;
```

```{julia}

#| output: false
# %% Sampler: NUTS
n_samples = 1000

chain_NUTS = sample(FS2000_loglik, NUTS(), n_samples, initial_params = FS2000.parameter_values)
```

```{julia}

# %% Inspecting Posterior, preparing for plot
using StatsPlots
paramlist = get_parameters(FS2000)
chain_NUTS_rn = replacenames(chain_NUTS, Dict(["parameters[$i]" for i in 1:length(paramlist)] .=> get_parameters(FS2000)))

describe(chain_NUTS_rn)
```

```{julia}

# %%
chain_NUTS_plot = plot(chain_NUTS_rn)

# %%
savefig(chain_NUTS_plot, "assets/plots/chain_NUTS_plot.png")

chain_NUTS_rn
```

![[../assets/plots/chain_NUTS_plot.png]]

... Nice, so we can do DSGE in Julia easily. What advantages are there over MATLAB, R, etc...?
Turing.jl is not the only "cutting-edge" sampling package...

# Parallel Tempering: Pigeons.jl, Hot/Cold chains, DSGE Identification

Recall Canova & Sala (2009):

Section 2.4, 2.5

when population identification failures stem from a flat or ridged objective function, _both_ classical and Bayesian methods are in trouble. Prior restrictions help with _sample_ identification problems (small-N noise) only if the prior genuinely encodes information from outside the dataset. But the combination of tightly specified priors plus auxiliary parameter-space restrictions can produce a well-behaved posterior even when the underlying population objective function is poorly behaved.

![[../../Pasted image 20260511154659.png]]

priors can reduce sample identification problems but cannot fix poorly conditioned _population_ mappings, and that "uncritical use of Bayesian methods, including employing prior distributions which do not truly reflect spread uncertainty, may hide identification pathologies."

## Parallel Tempering in a nutshell

Designed to not hide identification problems, which Bayesian methods usually do. Works best with high-dimensional, multimodal, and weakly identified models. 

![[../../Pasted image 20260511155135.png]]

Parallel tempering (PT) is a Markov chain Monte Carlo method that runs not one chain but a _ladder_ of chains targeting tempered versions of the posterior, indexed by an inverse temperature β ∈ [0, 1]. The β = 1 chain samples from the actual posterior; the β = 0 chain samples from the prior (the "reference"); intermediate chains sample from distributions that interpolate between the two, of the form π_β(θ) ∝ p(θ)^(1−β) · p(θ|data)^β. Chains exchange states periodically through Metropolis-Hastings swaps, so a sample that started exploring near the prior can flow upward toward the posterior, and vice versa. The result is a sampler that mixes well across multimodal or ridge-shaped posteriors that trap single-chain methods like NUTS, and that yields the marginal likelihood log Z as a free by-product of the tempering path — useful for formal Bayesian model comparison.

 - PT makes the prior-vs-data contribution observable
 - The communication barrier Λ flags poorly-identified geometry directly
 - The marginal likelihood is computed natively, not as an afterthought

Pigeons output:

scans   Λ      time(s)  allc(B)  log(Z₁/Z₀)  min(α)  mean(α)  min(αₑ)  mean(αₑ)
   8    1.47    249     1.54e+11    1.19e+03   0.542   0.836    0.224     0.525

- **Λ < 1**: prior and posterior are close — the data didn't move beliefs much. In DSGE-land, this is often a _bad_ sign: it can mean weak identification, or it can mean your prior was already correct (the latter is rare in practice).
- **Λ between 1 and 5**: typical for well-identified problems where data are informative.
- **Λ > 5**: a sharp posterior relative to the prior — could mean strong data, or could mean numerical pathology. Diagnose carefully.

## Meeting Notes

working papers --> current

1. Currency dominance, exchange rates. No research assistant, no overlap. Paper in revision stage. 
	1. Currency dominance specifically: in syllabus because GC in discussion (embryonal stage) too early. 
2. canova and sala: identification. 
	1. Topic for an econometrician. Anyone in mind? HEC? Can work alongside an econometrician. Co-supervisor. 

More space for assistant. 

### project:
- early stage
- 2 things
	- datawork to construct a new time series
	- VAR with identification strategy (new)
		- estimate, identification schemes on shocks
- Theme: technology adoption/AI shocks. what happens in economy when there are AI shocks
	- AI shocks == delta in production function, capital more productive than labour. what happens in economy? What should policy makers do?
- Current stage:
	- objective of empirical part: identify AI shocks, technology adoption in context of VAR. theory says we should look at capital share / labour share. renumeration to production input. Payments to capital should increase relative to labour. 

Involvement could be in one of two:
1. developing a measure of payment to capital as a % of output. capital share.
	1. construct this object at a quarterly frequency. Put it with VAR with other vars. Identify shocks in mind.
	2. Read: what GC sends. Barkai 2020. JF. He does this at an annual frequency. Need quarterly frequency. Using his methodology. His focus is more the long run + methodological frequency. GC has proxies, alternatives. 
	3. update Barkai's work, extend to quarterly frequency. Things are done up until here. Need to be understood, verified, stresstested, etc...

First part: learn about national accounts, how we make sense of economic data, etc... (code revision?) Code in Python. Simplify the code, optimize. Based off code from Barkai. Can read code in R as well. Would need to compare/contrast translation from original. 
	review code, code translation, with the economic data and accounts in mind
	understand how this code is computed and used at annual frequency. 

Reward (besides $ and work)
- separate application for my thesis potentially. Something similar/related for Canada specifically. Interaction mainly with Giacomo. Build up professional relationship with him.
- 10 - 15 hrs/week. 

Could get access to python code.
